questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-9}{2}=\frac{z-5}{1}$

$\mathrm{Q}(1, \mu, 2)$ satisfies the line L

$$ \therefore \frac{1-4}{1}=\frac{\mu-9}{2}=\frac{2-5}{1} $$

$$ \Rightarrow-3=\frac{\mu-9}{2}=-3 \quad \Rightarrow \mu=3 $$

D.R. of PQ : $\lambda-1,2-\mu, 1$

Since $\mathrm{PQ} \perp \mathrm{L} \Rightarrow(\lambda-1) .1+2(2-\mu)+1.1=0$

$$ \Rightarrow \lambda-2 \mu+4=0 \quad \therefore \lambda=2 $$

Now lines : $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z+4}{6}$ \&

$$ \frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y-3}{3}=\frac{z+5}{6} $$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { i.e. } \vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+t_1(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}) \\ & \& \vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+t_2(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})\end{aligned}$

Distance

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{|((\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})-(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})) \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})|}{|2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}|} \\ & =\frac{|-9 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-\hat{k}|}{7}=\frac{\sqrt{146}}{7} \end{aligned} $$

questions.questionText: Let $f:(1, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be a function defined as $f(x)=\frac{x-1}{x+1}$. Let $f^{i+1}(x)=f\left(f^i(x)\right), i=1,2, \ldots, 25$, where $f^1(x)=f(x)$. If $g(x)+f^{26}(x)=0, x \in(1, \infty)$, then the area of the region bounded by the curves $y=g(x), 2 y=2 x-3, y=0$ and $x=4$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We first focus on the orbit periodicity of $f^n(\boldsymbol{x})$ :

$$ f^1(x)=f(x)=\frac{x-1}{x+1} ; x \in(1, \infty) \ldots . . (i) $$

$f^2(x)=f\left\{f^1(x)\right\}=f\{f(x)\}=f\left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)=\frac{\left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)-1}{\left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)+1}=-\frac{2}{2 x}=-\frac{1}{x} ; x \in(1, \infty) \ldots . .(i i)$

$\boldsymbol{f}^3(\boldsymbol{x})=f\left\{\boldsymbol{f}^2(\boldsymbol{x})\right\}=f\left(-\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}}\right)=\frac{\left(-\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}}\right)-1}{\left(-\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}}\right)+1}=\frac{1+\boldsymbol{x}}{1-\boldsymbol{x}} ; \boldsymbol{x} \in(1, \infty) \ldots . .(\boldsymbol{i \boldsymbol { i i }})$

$f^4(x)=f\left\{f^3(x)\right\}=f\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)=\frac{\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)-1}{\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)+1}=\frac{2 x}{2}=x ; x \in(1, \infty) \ldots . .(i v)$

$$ f^5(x)=f\left\{f^4(x)\right\}=f(x)=\frac{x-1}{x+1} ; x \in(1, \infty) \ldots . . (v)$$

From the nature of table which completes the 25 iterations in 6 cycles with orbit periodicity 4 :

$$ f^{25}(x)=f(x)=f^1(x) \Rightarrow f^{26}(x)=f^2(x)=-\frac{1}{x} \ldots \ldots(v i) $$

Equation (vi) exactly identifies the function $\boldsymbol{g}(\boldsymbol{x})$ defined under SOW :

$$ \boldsymbol{g}(\boldsymbol{x})+\boldsymbol{f}^{26}(\boldsymbol{x})=0 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{g}(\boldsymbol{x})=-\boldsymbol{f}^{26}(\boldsymbol{x})=\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}} \ldots \ldots(v i i) $$

Equation (vii) has identified the function $\boldsymbol{g}(\boldsymbol{x})$ and we can mathematically plot the bounded area between the curves :

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline g(x) & \text { Slant line } & \text { Horizontal line } & \text { Vertical line } \\ \hline \frac{1}{x} & y=x-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) & X \text {-axis } & x=4 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Pair } & \boldsymbol{g}(\boldsymbol{x}) & \text { Slant line } & \text { Feasible } & \text { Intersection Points } \\ \hline 1 & \frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}} & \boldsymbol{y}=\boldsymbol{x}-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) & \text { Yes } & \boldsymbol{A}, \boldsymbol{K} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{x}=x-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \Rightarrow 2 x^2-3 x-2=0 \Rightarrow x=\left(2,-\frac{1}{2}\right) \Rightarrow y=\left(\frac{1}{2},-2\right)$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Intersections } & \boldsymbol{A} & \boldsymbol{K} & \text { Discarded point } & \text { Reason } \\ \hline \text { Coordinates } & \left(2, \frac{1}{2}\right) & \left(-\frac{1}{2},-2\right) & \boldsymbol{K} & \text { Falls outside the scope of bounded area } \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Pair } & \boldsymbol{g}(\boldsymbol{x}) & \text { Vertical line } & \text { Feasible } & \text { Intersection Points } \\ \hline 2 & \frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}} & \boldsymbol{x}=4 & \text { Yes } & \boldsymbol{B} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$\Rightarrow x=4 \Rightarrow y=0.25$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text { Intersection } & \boldsymbol{B} \\ \hline \text { Coordinate } & (4,0.25) \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Pair } & \text { Horizontal line } & \text { Vertical line } & \text { Feasible } & \text { Intersection point } \\ \hline 3 & \boldsymbol{X} \text {-axis } & \boldsymbol{x}=4 & \text { Yes } & \boldsymbol{C} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$\Rightarrow x=4, y=0$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text { Intersection } & \boldsymbol{C} \\ \hline \text { Coordinate } & (4,0) \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Pair } & \text { Slant line } & \text { Horizontal line } & \text { Feasible } & \text { Intersection point } \\ \hline 4 & \boldsymbol{y}=x-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) & X \text {-axis } & \text { Yes } & D \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$\Rightarrow \boldsymbol{y}=0 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{x}=\frac{3}{2}$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text { Intersection } & \boldsymbol{D} \\ \hline \text { Coordinate } & \left(\frac{3}{2}, 0\right) \\ \hline \end{array} $$

questions.questionText: If $26\left(\frac{2^3}{3}\left({ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_2\right)+\frac{2^5}{5}\left({ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_4\right)+\frac{2^7}{7}\left({ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_6\right)+\cdots+\frac{2^{13}}{13}\left({ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_{12}\right)\right)=3^{13}-\alpha$, then $\alpha$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\boldsymbol{S}_{\boldsymbol{r}}=26\left[\left(\frac{2^3}{3}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_2\right)+\left(\frac{2^5}{5}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_4\right)+\left(\frac{2^7}{7}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_6\right)+\left(\frac{2^9}{9}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_8\right)+\ldots .+\left(\frac{2^{13}}{13}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_{12}\right)\right]$

$=26\left[\sum\limits_{r=1}^6\left(\frac{2^{2 r+1}}{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_{2 r}\right)\right]$.

$\boldsymbol{S}_{\boldsymbol{r}}=\frac{26}{13}\left[\sum\limits_{r=1}^6\left(2^{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_{2 r+1}\right)\right]$

$=2\left[\sum\limits_{r=0}^6\left(2^{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_{2 r+1}\right)-\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_1\right) \cdot 2^1\right]$

$=2\left[\sum\limits_{r=0}^6\left(2^{2 r+1}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{13} C_{2 r+1}\right)\right]-52$

$\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{S}_{\boldsymbol{r}} & =2\left[\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_1\right) \cdot 2^1+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_3\right) \cdot 2^3+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_5\right) \cdot 2^5+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_7\right) \cdot 2^7+\ldots . .+\left({ }^{13} \boldsymbol{C}_{13}\right) \cdot 2^{13}\right]-52 \\ & =(1+2)^{13}-(1-2)^{13}-52 \\ & =3^{13}+1-52 \\ & =3^{13}-51.....(i)\end{aligned}$

$$ \boldsymbol{S}_r=26\left[\left(\frac{2^3}{3}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_2\right)+\left(\frac{2^5}{5}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_4\right)+\left(\frac{2^7}{7}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_6\right)+\left(\frac{2^9}{9}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_8\right)+\ldots .+\left(\frac{2^{13}}{13}\right) \cdot\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_{12}\right)\right]=3^{13}-\boldsymbol{\alpha} \ldots . (ii)$$

From the equivalence relationship between equation $(\boldsymbol{i})$ and $(\boldsymbol{i i})$ we conclude $\alpha=51$

questions.questionText: The value of the integral $\int\limits_0^2 \frac{\sqrt{x\left(x^2+x+1\right)}}{(\sqrt{x+1})\left(\sqrt{x^4+x^2+1}\right)} \mathrm{d} x$ is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$I=\int\limits_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x\left(x^2+x+1\right)}}{(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^4+x^2+1}\right)}\right] d x$

$\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x\left(x^2+x+1\right)}}{(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^4+2 x^2+1-x^2}\right)}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x\left(x^2+x+1\right)}}{(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{\left(x^2+1\right)^2-x^2}\right)}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x\left(x^2+x+1\right)}}{\left.(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left\{\sqrt{\left(x^2+1+x\right)\left(x^2+1-x\right)}\right\}\right]}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{(\sqrt{x}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^2+x+1}\right)}{(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^2+1+x}\right) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^2+1-x}\right)}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x}}{(\sqrt{x+1}) \cdot\left(\sqrt{x^2-x+1}\right)}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{(x+1)\left(x^2-x+1\right)}}\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^2\left[\frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x^3+1}}\right] d x\end{aligned}$

Let $x=t^2 \Rightarrow d x=2 t d t$

$\boldsymbol{I}=\int_0^{\sqrt{2}}\left[\frac{2 t^2}{\sqrt{t^6+1}}\right] d t=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \int_0^{\sqrt{2}}\left[\frac{3 t^2}{\sqrt{t^6+1}}\right] d t$

Let $t^3=u \Rightarrow 3 t^2 d t=d u$

$\boldsymbol{I}=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 \cdot \int_0^{2 \sqrt{2}}\left[\frac{\boldsymbol{d u}}{\sqrt{u^2+1}}\right]$

Using standard integration formulaes :

$\int\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}}\right) \boldsymbol{d x}=\ln \left|\boldsymbol{x}+\sqrt{\boldsymbol{x}^2+\boldsymbol{a}^2}\right|+C$

= $\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left|\boldsymbol{u}+\sqrt{\boldsymbol{u}^2+1}\right|\right]_0^{2 \sqrt{2}}$

$=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \cdot[\ln (2 \sqrt{2}+3)-\ln 1]=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \cdot[\ln (3+2 \sqrt{2})]$

questions.questionText: Let $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{1}{3}, & x \leq \pi / 2 \\ \frac{\mathrm{~b}(1-\sin x)}{(\pi-2 x)^2}, & x>\pi / 2\end{array}\right.$. If $f$ is continuous at $x=\pi / 2$, then the value of $\int\limits_0^{3 \mathrm{~b}-6}\left|x^2+2 x-3\right| \mathrm{d} x$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
The function $\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})$ is continuous at $\boldsymbol{x}=\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}$ where

$\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\frac{1}{3} & ; x \leqslant \frac{\pi}{2} \\ \frac{b(1-\sin x)}{(\boldsymbol{\pi}-2 x)^2} & ; x>\frac{\pi}{2}\end{array} \cdots(i)\right.$.

We observe the LHL from equation $(i)$ :

$$ \lim\limits_{x \rightarrow\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{-}} f(x)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{3} \ldots \ldots(i i) $$

We must have the RHL from equation (i) to be $\frac{1}{3}$ for the function $\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})$ to be continuous at $\boldsymbol{x}=\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}$ :

$$ \lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{x} \rightarrow\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}\right)^{+}} \boldsymbol{f ( x )}=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \lim\limits _{\boldsymbol{x} \rightarrow\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}\right)^{+}} \frac{\boldsymbol{b}(1-\sin \boldsymbol{x})}{(\boldsymbol{\pi}-2 x)^2}=\frac{1}{3} \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{i i i}) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}\right)^{+}} \frac{\boldsymbol{b}(1-\sin \boldsymbol{x})}{(\boldsymbol{\pi}-2 \boldsymbol{x})^2}=\frac{0}{0}$ format $\leftarrow$ Apply L'hôpital's rule

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}\right)^{+}} \frac{b \cos \boldsymbol{x}}{4(\boldsymbol{\pi}-2 \boldsymbol{x})}=\frac{0}{0}$ format ค Apply L'hôpital's rule once more

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{+}} \frac{b \sin x}{8}=\frac{b}{8} \ldots \ldots(i v)$

We can mathematically demonstrate conditional continuity of $f(x)$ at $x=\frac{\pi}{2}$ from equation (iii) and (iv) :

$$ \frac{b}{8}=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow b=\frac{8}{3} \ldots \ldots(v) $$

Now, I = $\int\limits_0^{3 \mathrm{~b}-6}\left|x^2+2 x-3\right| \mathrm{d} x$

$=\int\limits_0^{(3)\left(\frac{8}{3}\right)-6}\left|\boldsymbol{x}^2+2 \boldsymbol{x}-3\right| \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{x}$

$=\int\limits_0^2\left|x^2+2 x-3\right| d x$

$=\int\limits_0^2|(x+3)(x-1)| d x$

$\left\{\begin{array}{l}(x+3)(x-1) \geqslant 0 ; x \in(-\infty,-3] \\ (x+3)(x-1) \leqslant 0 ; x \in[-3,1] \\ (x+3)(x-1) \geqslant 0 ; x \in[1, \infty)\end{array}\right.$

$=\int\limits_0^1(x+3)(1-x) d x$ $+\int\limits_1^2(x+3)(x-1) d x$

$=\int\limits_0^1\left(3-2 x-x^2\right) d x$ $+\int\limits_1^2\left(x^2+2 x-3\right) d x$

$\begin{aligned} & =\left[3 x-x^2-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^1 +\left[\frac{x^3}{3}+x^2-3 x\right]_1^2\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & =\left(3-1-\frac{1}{3}\right) +\left(\frac{8}{3}+4-6\right)-\left(\frac{1}{3}+1-3\right)\end{aligned}$

= 4

questions.questionText: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $x \sqrt{1-x^2} d y+\left(y \sqrt{1-x^2}-x \cos ^{-1} x\right) d x=0, x \in(0,1), \lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} y(x)=1$. Then $y\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$ equals :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\left(x \sqrt{1-x^2}\right) d y+\left(y \sqrt{1-x^2}-x \cos ^{-1} x\right) d x=0$

$\Rightarrow\left(x \sqrt{1-x^2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(y \sqrt{1-x^2}-x \cos ^{-1} x\right)=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\left(y \sqrt{1-x^2}-x \cos ^{-1} x\right)}{\left(x \sqrt{1-x^2}\right)}=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=\frac{\cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \ldots . $$(i)

Bernoulli's linear ODE of first order :

$\frac{d y}{d x}+P(x) \cdot y=Q(x)$ having $\boldsymbol{I} \cdot \boldsymbol{F}=\boldsymbol{e}^{\int \boldsymbol{P}(\boldsymbol{x}) \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{x}}$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \boldsymbol{P}(\boldsymbol{x}) & \boldsymbol{Q}(\boldsymbol{x}) \\ \hline \frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}} & \frac{\cos ^{-1} \boldsymbol{x}}{\sqrt{1-\boldsymbol{x}^2}} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I . F=e^{\int P(x) d x}=e^{\int \frac{d x}{x}}=e^{\ln x}=x \ldots . . (ii)$$

Apply the I.F from equation $(i i)$ in $\mathrm{ODE}(i)$ :

$$ \text { - } x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \Rightarrow x d y+y d x=\left(\frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) d x $$

$$ \Rightarrow d(x y)=\left(\frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) d x \Rightarrow \int d(x y)=\int\left(\frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) d x \ldots . .(iii) $$

We choose the first and second functions

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \boldsymbol{u} & \boldsymbol{v} \\ \hline \cos ^{-1} \boldsymbol{x} & \frac{\boldsymbol{x}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \\ \hline \end{array}\\ &\text { for the integrand in equation }(\boldsymbol{i} \boldsymbol{i} \boldsymbol{i}) \text { to proceed further: } \end{aligned} $$

$\begin{aligned} x y & =\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \int\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) d x+\int\left[\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) \cdot \int\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) d x\right] d x \\ & =-\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}-\int\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) \cdot\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right) d x \\ & =-\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}-\int\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right) \cdot\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right) d x \\ & =-\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}-\int d x \\ & =-\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}-x+C\end{aligned}$

$y(x)=\frac{C}{x}-1-\frac{\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}}{x} \ldots .(i v)$ where $C$ is the arbitrary constant

$\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} y(x)=\lim\limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}}\left[\frac{C}{x}-1-\frac{\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}}{x}\right]=C-1-\frac{(0) \cdot(0)}{1}=C-1 \ldots \ldots(v)$

We have the boundary condition

$\lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{x} \rightarrow 1^{-}} \boldsymbol{y}(\boldsymbol{x})=1 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{C}=2 \ldots . .(\boldsymbol{v} \boldsymbol{i}) \leftarrow$ From equation $(\boldsymbol{v})$

We have solved the ODE to reach a particular solution $(\boldsymbol{C}=2)$ from $(\boldsymbol{i v}) y(x)=\frac{2}{x}-1-\frac{\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \cdot \sqrt{1-x^2}}{x} \ldots$. (vii)

Substitute $\boldsymbol{x}=\frac{1}{2}$ in equation (vii) to evaluate $\boldsymbol{y}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=4-1-(\boldsymbol{\mathcal { 2 }}) \cdot\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{3}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\boldsymbol{\mathcal { 2 }}}\right)$

$=3-\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}$

questions.questionText: Let $\frac{x^2}{f\left(a^2+7 a+3\right)}+\frac{y^2}{f(3 a+15)}=1$ represent an ellipse with major axis along $y$-axis, where $f$ is a strictly decreasing positive function on $\mathbf{R}$. If the set of all possible values of $a$ is $\mathbf{R}-[\alpha, \beta]$, then $\alpha^2+\beta^2$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Since the ellipse has its major axis along the $y$-axis, the denominator under $y^2$ must be bigger than the denominator under $x^2$.

So we need

$$f(3a+15) > f\left(a^2+7a+3\right).$$

Given that $f$ is a strictly decreasing positive function, a bigger function value happens when the input is smaller. So,

$$3a+15 < a^2+7a+3.$$

Bring all terms to one side:

$$a^2+7a+3-3a-15>0$$

$$a^2+4a-12>0.$$

Complete the square:

$$a^2+4a-12=(a+2)^2-16.$$

So the inequality becomes

$$(a+2)^2-16>0 \;\Rightarrow\; (a+2)^2>16.$$

Taking square roots,

$$|a+2|>4 \;\Rightarrow\; a+2>4 \text{ or } a+2<-4.$$

So,

$$a>2 \text{ or } a<-6.$$

That means the allowed values are

$$a \in \mathbf{R}-[-6,2].$$

Hence, $\alpha=-6$ and $\beta=2$. Therefore,

$$\alpha^2+\beta^2=(-6)^2+(2)^2=36+4=40.$$

questions.questionText:
Let $\alpha=3 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{6}{11}\right)$ and $\beta=3 \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{9}\right)$, where inverse trigonometric functions take only the principal values.

Given below are two statements :

Statement I : $\quad \cos (\alpha+\beta)>0$.

Statement II : $\quad \cos (\alpha)<0$.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
$$ \text { Statement I is false but Statement II is true } $$
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\frac{1}{2}<\frac{6}{11}<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)<\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{6}{11}\right)<\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ ....(i)

$$ \Rightarrow 3 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)<3 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{6}{11}\right)<3 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2}<\alpha<\frac{3 \pi}{4} \Rightarrow \cos \alpha<0 \ldots . . (ii) $$

$\frac{4}{9}<\frac{1}{2}<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{9}\right)>\frac{\pi}{3}>\frac{\pi}{4}$

$$ \Rightarrow 3 \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{9}\right)>\pi>\frac{3 \pi}{4} \Rightarrow \beta>\pi \ldots \ldots (ii) $$

Inequalities $(i)$ and $(i \boldsymbol{i})$ shows the mathematical logic for $\alpha+\beta>\frac{\pi}{2}+\pi$

$\Rightarrow \cos (\boldsymbol{\alpha}+\boldsymbol{\beta})>0 \ldots \ldots$ (iii)

From equations (i) and (iii) we can conclude :

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Statement } & \text { I } & \text { II } \\ \hline \text { True } & \text { Yes } & \text { Yes } \\ \hline \end{array} $$

questions.questionText:
For the function $f(x)=\mathrm{e}^{\sin |x|}-|x|, x \in \mathbf{R}$, consider the following statements :

Statement I : $ f$ is differentiable for all $x \in \mathbf{R}$.

Statement II : $ f$ is increasing in $\left(-\pi,-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
Both Statement I and Statement II are true

Both Statement I and Statement II are false

Statement I is true but Statement II is false

Statement I is false but Statement II is true
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Here $f(x)=\mathrm{e}^{\sin |x|}-|x|$. Since $|x|$ is not differentiable at $x=0$, and also $\sin|x|$ has a “kink” at $x=0$, the function $f$ is not differentiable at $x=0$. So, Statement I is false.

Now take $x \in\left(-\pi,-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$. Here $x<0$, so $|x|=-x$ and hence $\sin|x|=\sin(-x)=-\sin x$. Therefore, on this interval we can write $f(x)=\mathrm{e}^{-\sin x}+x$ and differentiate to check whether $f$ is increasing.

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(x)=e^{-\sin x}+x \\\\ & f^{\prime}(x)=-e^{-\sin x} \cos x+1 \\\\ & \text { for } x \in\left(-\pi, \frac{-\pi}{2}\right) \quad \cos x<0 \\\\ & \therefore f^{\prime}(x)>0 \\\\ & \therefore f(x) \text { is increasing } \end{aligned} $$

Since $\cos x<0$ on $\left(-\pi,-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ and $e^{-\sin x}>0$, we get $-e^{-\sin x}\cos x>0$, so $f'(x)>0$ on this interval. Hence, Statement II is true.

questions.questionText:
If the system of linear equations :

$$ \begin{aligned} & x+y+z=6 \\ & x+2 y+5 z=10 \\ & 2 x+3 y+\lambda z=\mu \end{aligned} $$

has infinitely many solutions, then the value of $\lambda+\mu$ equals:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
To find the condition for infinitely many solutions, we first calculate the determinant $ D $ of the coefficients of $ x, y, z $:

$$ D=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 5 \\ 2 & 3 & \lambda \end{array}\right| =\lambda - 6 $$

For infinitely many solutions, $ D = 0 $. So, $ \lambda - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 6. $

Now, let us find $ D_1 $, the determinant obtained by replacing the first column with the constants:

$$ D_1=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & 1 & 1 \\ 10 & 2 & 5 \\ \mu & 3 & \lambda \end{array}\right| =2\lambda + 3\mu - 60 $$

Next, the determinant $ D_2 $ is obtained by replacing the second column with the constants:

$$ D_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 6 & 1 \\ 1 & 10 & 5 \\ 2 & \mu & \lambda \end{array}\right| =4(\lambda - \mu + 10) $$

Finally, $ D_3 $ is obtained by replacing the third column with the constants:

$$ D_3=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 6 \\ 1 & 2 & 10 \\ 2 & 3 & \mu \end{array}\right| =\mu - 16 $$

For the system to have infinitely many solutions, all the determinants must be zero:

$$ D = D_1 = D_2 = D_3 = 0 $$

Substitute $ \lambda = 6 $:

From $ D_3 = 0 $, $ \mu - 16 = 0 \Rightarrow \mu = 16. $

Thus,

$$ \lambda + \mu = 6 + 16 = 22 $$

questions.questionText: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}\alpha & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 5\end{array}\right]$ and $B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -5 \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \alpha & -2 \alpha\end{array}\right]+\operatorname{adj}(A)$. If $\operatorname{det}(B)=66$, then $\operatorname{det}(\operatorname{adj}(A))$ equals :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}\alpha & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 5\end{array}\right]$

$\Rightarrow|\boldsymbol{A}|=15 \boldsymbol{\alpha}-10+16=15 \boldsymbol{\alpha}+6$

Now adj $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}15 & 3 & -6 \\ -10 & 5 \alpha & 4 \\ 8 & -4 \alpha & 3 \alpha-2\end{array}\right]$

$\therefore \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -5 \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \alpha & -2 \alpha\end{array}\right]+\operatorname{adj}(\mathrm{A})$

$B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}16 & 3 & -6 \\ -10 & 0 & 4 \\ 8 & 0 & \alpha-2\end{array}\right]$

$\begin{aligned} & \&|\mathrm{~B}|=6(5 \alpha+6)=66 \text { (given in question) } \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=1 \\ & \Rightarrow|\mathrm{~A}|=21\end{aligned}$

$\boldsymbol{A} \cdot \operatorname{Adj}(\boldsymbol{A})=|\boldsymbol{A}| \boldsymbol{I} \Rightarrow|\boldsymbol{A} \cdot \operatorname{Adj}(\boldsymbol{A})|=\| \boldsymbol{A}|\boldsymbol{I}|=\left(|\boldsymbol{A}|^3\right)|\boldsymbol{I}|=|\boldsymbol{A}|^3$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|\boldsymbol{A}||\operatorname{Adj}(\boldsymbol{A})|=|\boldsymbol{A}|^3 \Rightarrow|\operatorname{Adj}(\boldsymbol{A})|=|\boldsymbol{A}|^2=(21)^2=441$

questions.questionText: A candidate has to go to the examination centre to appear in an examination. The candidate uses only one means of transportation for the entire distance out of bus, scooter and car. The probabilities of the candidate going by bus, scooter and car, respectively, are $\frac{2}{5}, \frac{1}{5}$ and $\frac{2}{5}$. The probabilities that the candidate reaches late at the examination centre are $\frac{1}{5}, \frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{1}{4}$ if the candidate uses bus, scooter and car, respectively. Given that the candidate reached late at the examination centre, the probability that the candidate travelled by bus is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Conveyance } & \text { Bus } & \text { Scooter } & \text { Car } & \text { Arrive late } & \text { Bus } & \text { Scooter } & \text { Car } \\ \hline \text { Probability } & \frac{2}{5} & \frac{1}{5} & \frac{2}{5} & \text { Probability } & \frac{1}{5} & \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{4} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { We label the events in the chart below : }\\ &\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Conveyance } & \text { Bus } & \text { Scooter } & \text { Car } & \text { Arrive late by } & \text { Bus } & \text { Scooter } & \text { Car } \\ \hline \text { Event } & B & S & C & \text { Event } & \boldsymbol{L}_b & L_s & \boldsymbol{L}_c \\ \hline \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

We label the event that the candidate arrives late be $\boldsymbol{L}$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text { Event } & \boldsymbol{B} & \boldsymbol{S} & \boldsymbol{C} & \boldsymbol{L}_b & \boldsymbol{L}_s & \boldsymbol{L}_c \\ \hline \text { Probability } & \frac{2}{5} & \frac{1}{5} & \frac{2}{5} & \frac{1}{5} & \frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{4} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$\Rightarrow P(L \cap B)=P(L) \cdot P(B / L)=P(B \cap L)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $P(B / L)=\frac{P(B \cap L)}{P(L)}$

$=\frac{P(B) \cdot P(L / B)}{P(B \cap L)+P(S \cap L)+P(C \cap L)}$

$=\frac{P(B) \cdot P(L / B)}{P(B) \cdot P(L / B)+P(S) \cdot P(L / S)+P(C) \cdot P(C / B)}$

$P(B / L)=\frac{P(B) \cdot P\left(L_b\right)}{P(B) \cdot P\left(L_b\right)+P(S) \cdot P\left(L_s\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(L_c\right)}$

$=\frac{\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{5}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)+\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)}$

$=\left(\frac{2}{25}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{300}{24+20+30}\right)=\frac{12}{37}$

questions.questionText: Let $\alpha=3+4+8+9+13+14+\ldots$ upto 40 terms. If $(\tan \beta)^{\frac{\alpha}{1020}}$ is a root of the equation $x^2+x-2=0, \beta \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$, then $\sin ^2 \beta+3 \cos ^2 \beta$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
1. 1. A series sum is defined : $\boldsymbol{\alpha}=3+4+8+9+13+14+\ldots . . \boldsymbol{t}_{40}$

  2. 2. $(\tan \boldsymbol{\beta})^{\frac{\alpha}{1020}}$ is a root of the quadratic equation $\boldsymbol{x}^2+\boldsymbol{x}-2=0$ where $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in\left(0, \frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{2}\right)$

  3. 3. To evaluate the expression $\sin ^2 \beta+3 \cos ^2 \beta$

$$\therefore $$ $\boldsymbol{\alpha}=3+4+8+9+13+14+\ldots . . \boldsymbol{t}_{40}$

$=7+17+27+\ldots . .+\boldsymbol{T}_{20}$

$=\frac{20}{2}\{(2) \cdot(7)+(20-1) \cdot(10)\}$

$=(10) \cdot(14+190)=2,040 \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{i})$

Equation (i) gives the value of $\alpha=2,040$ :

$$ (\tan \beta)^{\frac{\alpha}{1020}}=(\tan \beta)^{\frac{2040}{1020}}=\tan ^2 \beta \ldots \ldots(i i) $$

Now let us solve the quadratic equation $x^2+x-2=0$ :

$x^2+x-2=0 \Rightarrow x^2+2 x-x-2=0 \Rightarrow(x+2)(x-1)=0$

$$ \Rightarrow \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline p & q \\ \hline-2 & 1 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

where $\boldsymbol{p}$ and $\boldsymbol{q}$ are the roots

We have derived in equation $(i i)(\tan \beta)^{\frac{\alpha}{1020}}=\tan ^2 \beta$ which is also a root of the equation $x^2+x-2=0$ :

From the pair of roots we get $\tan ^2 \beta$ =1$ \Rightarrow \tan \beta= \pm 1 $ As $ \tan ^2 \beta \geqslant 0$ always

$\boldsymbol{\beta} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \Rightarrow \tan \boldsymbol{\beta}=1 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\beta}=\frac{\pi}{4}$

$\Rightarrow \sin ^2 \beta+3 \cos ^2 \beta=\sin ^2 \frac{\pi}{4}+3 \cos ^2 \frac{\pi}{4}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)=2$

questions.questionText:
Consider the relation $R$ on the set $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$ defined by $(a, b) \in R$ if and only if $1+a b>0$. Then, among the statements :

I. The number of elements in R is 17

II. R is an equivalence relation
options:
Only I is true

Only II is true

Both I and II are true

Neither I nor II is true
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We first observe the relation $\boldsymbol{R}$ defined on

$\boldsymbol{S} \times \boldsymbol{S} (\boldsymbol{a}, \boldsymbol{b})$ is an ordered pair $\in \boldsymbol{R}$ iff $(1+\boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b})>0=\boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b}>-1 \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{i})$

Refer the chart below :

a b ab ∈ R
−2 −2 4 Yes
−1 −1 2 Yes
0 0 0 Yes
1 1 2 Yes
2 2 4 Yes
−2 −1 2 Yes
..... ..... ..... Yes
..... ..... ..... Yes

We also observe the mathematical relationship from $(\boldsymbol{i})$ :

$a b>-1 \Rightarrow b a>-1 \equiv(a, b) \in R \Rightarrow(b, a) \in R \ldots \ldots(i i) \leftarrow$ symmetric

We take 2 ordered pair entries from $\boldsymbol{R}$ :

$$ \begin{array}{|r|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Ordered pair }(\boldsymbol{a}, \boldsymbol{b}) & (2,0) & \boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b}>-1 & (0,-2) & \boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b}>-1 & (2,-2) & \boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b} \ngtr-1 & \text { Transitive } \\ \hline \boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b} & 0 & 0>-1 & 0 & 0>-1 & -4 & -4<-1 & \\ \hline \in \boldsymbol{R} & \text { Yes } & & \text { Yes } & & \text { No } & & \text { No } \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$\therefore $$ $$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Relation } & \text { Reflexive } & \text { Symmetric } & \text { Transitive } \\ \hline \boldsymbol{R} & \text { Yes } & \text { Yes } & \text { No } \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Now let us focus on the cardinality of

$\boldsymbol{R} \in \boldsymbol{S} \times \boldsymbol{S} ;(\boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b}>-1)$

S R a b No of entries Cumulative Total
{ −2, −1, 0, 1, 2 } ab > −1 −2 ∈ { −2, −1, 0 } 3 3
{ −2, −1, 0, 1, 2 } ab > −1 −1 ∈ { −2, −1, 0 } 3 6
{ −2, −1, 0, 1, 2 } ab > −1 0 ∈ S 5 11
{ −2, −1, 0, 1, 2 } ab > −1 1 ∈ { 0, 1, 2 } 3 14
{ −2, −1, 0, 1, 2 } ab > −1 2 ∈ { 0, 1, 2 } 3 17

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline \mathrm{I} & \text { The number of elements in } \mathrm{R} \text { is } 17 & \text { Valid } & \text { Option } \\ \hline \mathrm{II} & \mathrm{R} \text { is an equivalence relation } & \text { Invalid } & \mathrm{A} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

questions.questionText: A set of four observations has mean 1 and variance 13. Another set of six observations has mean 2 and variance 1 . Then, the variance of all these 10 observations is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Observation } & \boldsymbol{x}_1 & \boldsymbol{x}_2 & \boldsymbol{x}_3 & \boldsymbol{x}_4 & \boldsymbol{n} & \text { Observation } & \boldsymbol{y}_1 & \boldsymbol{y}_2 & \boldsymbol{y}_3 & \boldsymbol{y}_4 & \boldsymbol{y}_5 & \boldsymbol{y}_6 & \boldsymbol{n} \\ \hline \text { Set } & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 4 & \text { Set } & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 6 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \text { Set } 1 \rightrightarrows \mu_x=\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^4 x_i}{4}=1 \ldots . (i) $$

$\sigma_x^2=\left\lfloor\left(\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^4 x_i^2}{4}\right)-\mu_x^2\right\rfloor=13 \ldots \ldots(i i)$

Set $2 \rightrightarrows \mu_y=\frac{\sum\limits_{j=1}^6 y_j}{6}=2 \ldots \ldots(i i i)$

$\sigma_y^2=\left[\left(\frac{\sum\limits_{j=1}^6 y_j^2}{6}\right)-\mu_y^2\right]=1 \ldots \ldots(i v)$

We mathematically extract from equations $(i)$ and $(i i)$ :

$$ \sum\limits_{i=1}^4 x_i=4 \ldots .(\boldsymbol{v}) \quad \sum\limits_{i=1}^4 x_i^2=4\left(\mu_x^2+13\right)=4(1+13)=56 \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{v} \boldsymbol{i}) $$

We mathematically extract from equations (iii) and (iv) :

$$ \sum\limits_{j=1}^6 \boldsymbol{y}_j=12 \ldots .(\boldsymbol{v i i}) \quad \sum\limits_{j=1}^6 y_j^2=6\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_y^2+1\right)=6(4+1)=30 \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{v i i i}) $$

We have mathematically compiled a summary of all the information in equations (v) through (viii) to compute the mean and the variance for the 10 datapoints in set $1 \cup$ set 2 :

$\mu_t=\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^4 x_i+\sum\limits_{j=1}^6 y_j}{4+6}=\frac{4+12}{10}=1.6 \ldots . .(\boldsymbol{i x})$

$\sigma_t^2=\left[\left(\frac{\sum_{i=1}^4 \boldsymbol{x}_i^2+\sum_{j=1}^6 \boldsymbol{y}_j^2}{4+6}\right)-\boldsymbol{\mu}_t^2\right]$

$=\frac{56+30}{4+6}-\begin{gathered} (1.6)^2\end{gathered}$

$=8.6-2.56=6.04$

questions.questionText: If a straight line drawn through the point of intersection of the lines $4 x+3 y-1=0$ and $3 x+4 y-1=0$, meets the co-ordinate axes at the points P and Q , then the locus of the mid point of PQ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
1. 1. A straight line $\boldsymbol{L}$ passes through the point of intersection of 2 straight lines: $\left\{\begin{array}{l}\boldsymbol{L}_1: 4 \boldsymbol{x}+3 \boldsymbol{y}-1=0 \\ \boldsymbol{L}_2: 3 \boldsymbol{x}+4 \boldsymbol{y}-1=0\end{array}\right.$

  2. 2. The straight line $\boldsymbol{L}$ cuts the axes at $\boldsymbol{P}$ and $\boldsymbol{Q}$

  3. 3. To identify the locus of the mid-point of the segment $P Q$

  1. The point of intersection of $\boldsymbol{L}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{L}_2$ is $\boldsymbol{I}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}, \boldsymbol{\beta})$

  2. The mid-point of the segment $\boldsymbol{P Q}$ is $\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{h}, \boldsymbol{k})$

Analytical Study :

We first solve the pair of straight lines $\boldsymbol{L}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{L}_2$ to locate the point of intersection :

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4 x+3 y-1=0 \Rightarrow 12 x+9 y=3 \\ & 3 x+4 y-1=0 \Rightarrow 12 x+16 y=4 \end{aligned} \quad \Rightarrow \beta=\frac{1}{7} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\alpha}=\frac{1}{7} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{I}\left(\frac{1}{7}, \frac{1}{7}\right) \ldots . . (i)$$

The straight line $\boldsymbol{L}$ passes through the point $\boldsymbol{I}$ identified by equation (i)

The equation of the straight line $\boldsymbol{L}$ in point slope form is written below :

$$ L:\left(y-\frac{1}{7}\right)=m\left(x-\frac{1}{7}\right) \equiv L: 7 m x-7 y=m-1 \equiv L: \frac{7 m x}{m-1}+\frac{7 y}{1-m}=1 \ldots . (ii)$$

The equation (ii) gives the straight line $\boldsymbol{L}$ in intercept form where the $\boldsymbol{X}$ and $\boldsymbol{Y}$ intercepts are observed below :

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \boldsymbol{P} & \boldsymbol{Q} \\ \hline\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{m}-1}{7 \boldsymbol{m}}, 0\right) & \left(0, \frac{1-\boldsymbol{m}}{7}\right) \\ \hline \end{array}\text { ..... Chart } 1 \end{aligned} $$

From Chart 1, the mid-point of the section $\boldsymbol{P Q}$ is derived applying mid-point formulae :

$$ M(h, k)=M\left(\frac{m-1}{14 m}, \frac{1-m}{14}\right) \equiv \begin{array}{|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Point } & h & k \\ \hline M(h, k) & \frac{m-1}{14 m} & \frac{1-m}{14} \\ \hline \end{array} \quad \ldots . \text { Chart } 2 $$

Chart 2 has the $\boldsymbol{X}$ and $\boldsymbol{Y}$ coordinate each as a function of $\boldsymbol{m}$ where we can eliminate $\boldsymbol{m}$ and mathematically trace the locus :

$$ \boldsymbol{k}=\frac{1-\boldsymbol{m}}{14} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{m}=1-14 \boldsymbol{k} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{h}=\frac{1-14 \boldsymbol{k}-1}{14(1-14 \boldsymbol{k})}=\frac{14 \boldsymbol{k}}{14(14 \boldsymbol{k}-1)}=\frac{\boldsymbol{k}}{(14 \boldsymbol{k}-1)} \Rightarrow 14 \boldsymbol{h} \boldsymbol{k}-\boldsymbol{h}=\boldsymbol{k} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{h}+\boldsymbol{k}-14 \boldsymbol{h} \boldsymbol{k}=0 $$

The locus of the mid-point of the section $\boldsymbol{P Q}$ after replacement of the dummy variables is found to be the rectangular hyperbola $\boldsymbol{x}+\boldsymbol{y}-14 \boldsymbol{x} \boldsymbol{y}=0 $

questions.questionText:
Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}=10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and a vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ be such that $2(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})+3(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=\overrightarrow{0}$.

If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=15$, then $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
The unknown vector $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}$ is represented in 3D: $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}=\boldsymbol{x} \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\boldsymbol{y} \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+z \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$

Cross product of 2 vectors $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{a}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}}$ :

$$ (\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}})=\left|\begin{array}{l|r|r} \hat{\boldsymbol{i}} & \hat{\boldsymbol{j}} & \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \tag{i}\\ 4 & -1 & 3 \\ 10 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right|=-5 \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+34 \hat{j}+18 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \ldots . . (i)$$

Cross product of 2 vectors $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}$ :

$$ (\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}})=\left|\begin{array}{l|l|r} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 10 & 2 & -1 \\ \boldsymbol{x} & \boldsymbol{y} & \boldsymbol{z} \end{array}\right|=(2 z+y) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-(10 z+\boldsymbol{x}) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(10 \boldsymbol{y}-2 \boldsymbol{x}) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{i} \boldsymbol{i}) $$

We know, $2(\boldsymbol{a} \times \boldsymbol{b})+3(\boldsymbol{b} \times \boldsymbol{c})=0$

$\Rightarrow 2(-5 \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+34 \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+18 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}})+3\{(2 \boldsymbol{z}+\boldsymbol{y}) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-(10 \boldsymbol{z}+x) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(10 \boldsymbol{y}-2 x) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}\}=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $(6 z+3 y-10) \hat{i}+(68-30 z-3 x) j+(36+30 y-6 x) \hat{k}=0$

As individual vector components of a null vector are zero.

$\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{x}-5 \boldsymbol{y} & =6 \ldots . \boldsymbol{P}_1 \\ \boldsymbol{y}+2 \boldsymbol{z} & =\frac{10}{3} \ldots . . \boldsymbol{P}_2 \\ 10 \boldsymbol{z}+\boldsymbol{x} & =\frac{68}{3} \ldots . \boldsymbol{P}_3\end{aligned}$

$(\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{c})=15 \Rightarrow(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k})=4 x-y+3 z=15 \ldots \ldots(i v)$

Equation set $(i i i)$ is mathematically constructed to form a linear combination of 2 planes $\boldsymbol{P}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{P}_2$ generating the $3^{\text {rd }}$ plane $\boldsymbol{P}_3$ :

$$ \boldsymbol{x}-5 \boldsymbol{y}+(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) \cdot(\boldsymbol{y}+2 \boldsymbol{z})=\frac{68}{3} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\lambda}=5 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{P}_1+5 \boldsymbol{P}_2=\boldsymbol{P}_3 \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{v}) $$

Equation ( $\boldsymbol{v}$ ) is conclusive in generating a family of planes $\left\{\boldsymbol{P}_1, \boldsymbol{P}_2, \boldsymbol{P}_3\right\}$ indicating infinite solutions for the system equation (iii)

We need an auxiliary equation provided through (iv) to render an unique solution for the vector $\vec{c}$ :

$\left[\begin{array}{rlrr}x-5 y & =6 & \ldots . & P_1 \\ y+2 z & =\frac{10}{3} & \ldots . & P_2 \\ 4 x-y+3 z & =15 & \ldots . & P_4\end{array}\right] \ldots .(v i)$

Equation (vi) can be solved using Determinants and Cramer's rule :

$$ \boldsymbol{\Delta}=\left|\begin{array}{r|r|r} 1 & -5 & 0 \tag{vii}\\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 4 & -1 & 3 \end{array}\right| \quad \leftarrow \quad \boldsymbol{R}_3=\boldsymbol{R}_3-4 \boldsymbol{R}_1=\left|\begin{array}{r|r|r} 1 & -5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 19 & 3 \end{array}\right|=-35 \quad \ldots . . (vii)$$

$\Delta_x=\left|\begin{array}{|l|r|l|}6 & -5 & 0 \\ \frac{10}{3} & 1 & 2 \\ 15 & -1 & 3\end{array}\right|$

$\begin{aligned} & \boldsymbol{R}_1=\boldsymbol{R}_1+5 \boldsymbol{R}_2 \\ & \boldsymbol{R}_3=\boldsymbol{R}_3+\boldsymbol{R}_2\end{aligned}$

$=\left|\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\frac{68}{3} & 0 & 10 \\ \frac{10}{3} & 1 & 2 \\ \frac{55}{3} & 0 & 5\end{array}\right|$

$C_1 \leftrightarrows C_2$

$=-\left|\begin{array}{l|l}0 & \frac{68}{3} \\ 1 & \frac{10}{3} \\ 0 & \frac{10}{3} \\ \frac{55}{3}\end{array}\right|=\left(\frac{68}{3}\right) \cdot(5)-\left(\frac{55}{3}\right) \cdot(10)=-70 \ldots \ldots(v i i i)$

Equations $(\boldsymbol{v i i}),(\boldsymbol{v i i i}),(\boldsymbol{i x})$ and $(\boldsymbol{x})$ provides the solution to system equation $(\boldsymbol{v i})$ from Cramer's rule :

$\left[\begin{array}{l}x=\frac{\Delta_x}{\Delta}=\frac{70}{35}= \\ y=\frac{\Delta_y}{\Delta}=-\frac{28}{35}=-\frac{4}{5} \\ z=\frac{\Delta_z}{\Delta}=\frac{217}{(3) \cdot(35)}=\frac{31}{15}\end{array}\right]$

$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}=(2) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+\left(\frac{31}{15}\right) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$

$\begin{aligned} & \therefore(\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}) \cdot(\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}) \\ & =\left\{(2) \hat{i}-\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \hat{j}+\left(\frac{31}{15}\right) \hat{k}\right\} \cdot(\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}) \\ & =2-\frac{4}{5}-\frac{31}{5}\end{aligned}$

$=-\frac{4+31-10}{5} =-5$

questions.questionText:
Let a line $L_1$ pass through the origin and be perpendicular to the lines

$\mathrm{L}_2: \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(3+\mathrm{t}) \hat{i}+(2 \mathrm{t}-1) \hat{j}+(2 \mathrm{t}+4) \hat{k}$ and

$\mathrm{L}_3: \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(3+2 \mathrm{~s}) \hat{i}+(3+2 \mathrm{~s}) \hat{j}+(2+\mathrm{s}) \hat{k}, \mathrm{t}, \mathrm{s} \in \mathbf{R}$.

If $(a, b, c), a \in \mathbf{Z}$, is the point on $\mathrm{L}_3$ at a distance of $\sqrt{17}$ from the point of intersection of $\mathrm{L}_1$ and $\mathrm{L}_2$, then $(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})^2$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
A pair of lines in 3D have the vector equations :

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|} \hline \boldsymbol{L}_2 & \boldsymbol{L}_3 & (\boldsymbol{t}, s) \\ \hline \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{r}}=(3+\boldsymbol{t}) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+(2 \boldsymbol{t}-1) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(2 \boldsymbol{t}+4) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} & \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{r}}=(3+2 \boldsymbol{s}) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+(3+2 \boldsymbol{s}) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(2+\boldsymbol{s}) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} & \in \mathbb{R} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

A third line $\boldsymbol{L}_1$ passes through the origin and is ⟂ to both the lines $\boldsymbol{L}_2$ and $\boldsymbol{L}_3$ defined just above

The point of intersection of $\boldsymbol{L}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{L}_2$ is at a distance $\sqrt{17}$ units from another point with coordinates $(\boldsymbol{a}, \boldsymbol{b}, \boldsymbol{c})$ and lying on $\boldsymbol{L}_3$ where $a \in Z$

$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{L}_2: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-4}{2}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{L}_5: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-2}{1}=\mathrm{s} \\ & \text { vector } \perp \text { to } \mathrm{L}_2 \& \mathrm{~L}_3\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{lll}i & j & k \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|=\hat{i}(-2)-\hat{j}(-3)+\hat{k}(-2) \\ & =-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} \\ & =-(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})\end{aligned}$

Now, $\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2}=\ell$

intersection of $\mathrm{L}_1$ \& $\mathrm{L}_2$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}+3=2 \ell, 2 \mathrm{t}-1=-3 \ell, 2 \mathrm{t}+4=2 \ell $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $t+3=2 t+4$

$$ \begin{aligned} & t=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow P \equiv(2,-3,2) \end{aligned} $$

A point on $\mathrm{L}_3$ is $\mathrm{Q}(2 \mathrm{~s}+3,2 \mathrm{~s}+3, \mathrm{~s}+2)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow P Q^2=(2 s+1)^2+(2 s+6)^2+s^2=17 \\ & \Rightarrow 9 s^2+28 s+20=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(9 s+10)(s+2)=0 \Rightarrow s=-2 \\ & \therefore Q \equiv(-1,-1,0) \equiv(a, b, c) \Rightarrow(a+b+c)^2=4 \end{aligned} $$

questions.questionText: Consider the circle C : $x^2+y^2-6 x-8 y-11=0$. Let a variable chord AB of the circle C subtend a right angle at the origin. If the locus of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the origin on the chord AB is the circle $x^2+y^2-\alpha x-\beta y-\gamma=0$, then $\alpha+\beta+2 \gamma$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Assumptions :

  1. $\boldsymbol{F}(h, \boldsymbol{k})$ is the foot of the ⟂ drop from origin on the variable chord $\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{B}$

  2. The end-points of the chord $\boldsymbol{A B}$ are

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \boldsymbol{A} & \boldsymbol{B} \\ \hline\left(\boldsymbol{x}_1, \boldsymbol{y}_1\right) & \left(\boldsymbol{x}_2, \boldsymbol{y}_2\right) \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Circle } & \text { Equation } & \text { Transform } & \text { Centre } & \text { Radius } \\ \hline \boldsymbol{C} & \boldsymbol{x}^2+\boldsymbol{y}^2-6 \boldsymbol{x}-8 \boldsymbol{y}-11=0 & (\boldsymbol{x}-3)^2+(\boldsymbol{y}-4)^2=36 & (3,4) & 6 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

We will now form the line equations of the ⟂ drop $\boldsymbol{O F}$ and the variable chord $\boldsymbol{A B}$ :

$$ l_{O F}: y=\left(\frac{k}{h}\right) x \Rightarrow l_{A B}: y=-\left(\frac{h}{k}\right) x+c ....(i) $$

The foot of the ⟂ on line $\boldsymbol{l}_{\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{B}}$ defined in equation (i) is $\boldsymbol{F}(\boldsymbol{h}, \boldsymbol{k})$ :

$$ k=-\left(\frac{\boldsymbol{h}}{\boldsymbol{k}}\right) \cdot(\boldsymbol{h})+c \Rightarrow c=\frac{\boldsymbol{h}^2+k^2}{k} \Rightarrow l_{A B}: \boldsymbol{h} \boldsymbol{x}+\boldsymbol{k} \boldsymbol{y}=h^2+k^2 \ldots \ldots (ii) $$

Equation (ii) which gives the equation of the variable chord $\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{B}$ cuts the circle at $\boldsymbol{A}\left(\boldsymbol{x}_1, \boldsymbol{y}_1\right)$ and $\boldsymbol{B}\left(\boldsymbol{x}_2, \boldsymbol{y}_2\right)$ is substituted in the equation of the circle $\boldsymbol{C}$ from chart 1 to form 2 quadratics in $\boldsymbol{x}$ and $\boldsymbol{y}$ respectively :

Quadratic in $x$ :

  • $\boldsymbol{x}^2+\boldsymbol{y}^2-6 \boldsymbol{x}-8 \boldsymbol{y}-11=0 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{k}^2 x^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2 \boldsymbol{y}^2-6 \boldsymbol{k}^2 \boldsymbol{x}-8 \boldsymbol{k}^2 \boldsymbol{y}-11 \boldsymbol{k}^2=0$

  • $\boldsymbol{k}^2 \boldsymbol{x}^2+\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2-\boldsymbol{h} \boldsymbol{x}\right)^2-6 \boldsymbol{k}^2 \boldsymbol{x}-8 \boldsymbol{k}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2-\boldsymbol{h} \boldsymbol{x}\right)-11 \boldsymbol{k}^2=0$

  • $\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right) \boldsymbol{x}^2-2 \boldsymbol{x}\left\{\boldsymbol{h}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)+3 \boldsymbol{k}^2-4 \boldsymbol{h} \boldsymbol{k}\right\}+\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)^2-8 \boldsymbol{k}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)-11 \boldsymbol{k}^2=0 \leftarrow$ Quadratic in $\boldsymbol{x}$

  • $\boldsymbol{x}_1 \boldsymbol{x}_2=\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)^2-8 \boldsymbol{k}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)-11 \boldsymbol{k}^2}{\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)} \ldots$. (iii) where $\boldsymbol{x}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{x}_2$ are the roots of the quadratic

Quadratic in $y$ :

  • $\boldsymbol{x}^2+\boldsymbol{y}^2-6 \boldsymbol{x}-8 \boldsymbol{y}-11=0 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{h}^2 \boldsymbol{x}^2+\boldsymbol{h}^2 \boldsymbol{y}^2-6 \boldsymbol{h}^2 \boldsymbol{x}-8 \boldsymbol{h}^2 \boldsymbol{y}-11 \boldsymbol{h}^2=0$

  • $\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2-\boldsymbol{k} \boldsymbol{y}\right)^2+\boldsymbol{h}^2 \boldsymbol{y}^2-6 \boldsymbol{h}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2-\boldsymbol{k} \boldsymbol{y}\right)-8 \boldsymbol{h}^2 \boldsymbol{y}-11 \boldsymbol{h}^2=0$

  • $\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right) \boldsymbol{y}^2-2 \boldsymbol{y}\left\{\boldsymbol{k}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)+4 \boldsymbol{h}^2-3 \boldsymbol{h} \boldsymbol{k}\right\}+\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+k^2\right)^2-6 \boldsymbol{h}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+k^2\right)-11 \boldsymbol{h}^2=0$ Quadratic in $\boldsymbol{y}$

  • $\boldsymbol{y}_1 \boldsymbol{y}_2=\frac{\left(h^2+k^2\right)^2-6 h\left(h^2+k^2\right)-11 h^2}{h^2+k^2} \ldots .(\boldsymbol{i v})$ where $\boldsymbol{y}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{y}_2$ are the roots of the quadratic

The variable chord $\boldsymbol{A B}$ with end-points $\boldsymbol{A}\left(\boldsymbol{x}_1, \boldsymbol{y}_1\right)$ and $\boldsymbol{B}\left(\boldsymbol{x}_2, \boldsymbol{y}_2\right)$ subtend a right $\measuredangle$ at origin.

We now infer from equations (iii) and (iv) :

$\left(\frac{y_1}{x_1}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{y_2}{x_2}\right)=-1$

$\Rightarrow \frac{\left\{\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)^2-6 \boldsymbol{h}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)-11 \boldsymbol{h}^2}{\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2}\right\}}{\left\{\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)^2-8 \boldsymbol{k}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)-11 \boldsymbol{k}^2}{\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)}\right\}}=-1$

$\Rightarrow \frac{\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)^2-6 \boldsymbol{h}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)-11 \boldsymbol{h}^2}{\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)^2-8 \boldsymbol{k}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)-11 \boldsymbol{k}^2}=-1$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)^2-6 \boldsymbol{h}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)-11 \boldsymbol{h}^2=-\left\{\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)^2-8 \boldsymbol{k}\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)-11 \boldsymbol{k}^2\right\} $$

  • $$\Rightarrow $$ $2\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)^2-\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)(6 \boldsymbol{h}+8 \boldsymbol{k})-11\left(\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2\right)=0$

  • $$\Rightarrow $$ $2\left(h^2+k^2\right) \cdot\left(h^2+k^2-3 h-4 k-\frac{11}{2}\right)=0$

  • $$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2-3 \boldsymbol{h}-4 \boldsymbol{k}-\frac{11}{2}=0 \leftarrow \boldsymbol{h}^2+\boldsymbol{k}^2 \neq 0[\because \boldsymbol{F}(\boldsymbol{h}, \boldsymbol{k})$ is not the origin $]$

  • $$\Rightarrow $$ $x^2+y^2-3 x-4 y-\frac{11}{2}=0 \ldots . .(\boldsymbol{v}) \leftarrow$ Locus of $F(h, k)$

The circle $C: \boldsymbol{x}^2+\boldsymbol{y}^2-\boldsymbol{\alpha} \boldsymbol{x}-\boldsymbol{\beta} \boldsymbol{y}-\boldsymbol{\lambda}=0$ be the locus of $\boldsymbol{F}(\boldsymbol{h}, \boldsymbol{k})$ which must be equivalent to equation $(\boldsymbol{v})$ :

$$\therefore $$ $\boldsymbol{x}^2+\boldsymbol{y}^2-\boldsymbol{\alpha} \boldsymbol{x}-\boldsymbol{\beta} \boldsymbol{y}-\boldsymbol{\lambda} \equiv \boldsymbol{x}^2+\boldsymbol{y}^2-3 \boldsymbol{x}-4 \boldsymbol{y}-\frac{11}{2}$

$$ \Rightarrow \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \boldsymbol{\alpha} & \boldsymbol{\beta} & \boldsymbol{\lambda} & 2 \boldsymbol{\lambda} & \boldsymbol{\alpha}+\boldsymbol{\beta}+2 \boldsymbol{\lambda} \\ \hline 3 & 4 & \frac{11}{2} & 11 & 18 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

questions.questionText: If $\int\limits_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 4}\left(\cot \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cot \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+1\right) d x=\alpha \log _{\mathrm{e}}(\sqrt{3}-1)$, then $9 \alpha^2$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\boldsymbol{I}=\int\limits_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\cot \left(\boldsymbol{x}-\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{3}\right) \cdot \cot \left(\boldsymbol{x}+\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{3}\right)+1\right] \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{x}$

$\begin{aligned} & =\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left|\frac{\cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}{\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right| d x \\ & =\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left|\frac{\cos \left\{\left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)-\left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right\}}{\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right| d x \\ & =\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right] d x\end{aligned}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $I=2 \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{2 \sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right] d x$

$\begin{aligned} & =2 \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{\left\{\cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right\}-\left\{\cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)-\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right]} d x\right. \\ & =2 \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)-\cos (2 x)}\right] d x\end{aligned}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{I}=-\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{-\frac{1}{2}-\cos (2 x)}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2}{1+2 \cos (2 x)}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2}{1+2\left(1-2 \sin ^2 x\right)}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2}{\left.3-4 \sin ^2 x\right)}\right] d x$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{I}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2 \sec ^2 x}{3 \sec ^2 x-4 \tan ^2 x}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2 \sec ^2 x}{3\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right)-4 \tan ^2 x}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2 \sec ^2 x}{3-\tan ^2 x}\right] d x$

Substitute $\boldsymbol{t}=\tan \boldsymbol{x} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{t}=\left(\sec ^2 \boldsymbol{x}\right) \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{x}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $I=\int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{2}{3-t^2}\right] d t=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{2 \sqrt{3}}{(\sqrt{3}+t)(\sqrt{3}-t)}\right] d t$

$=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{(\sqrt{3}+t)+(\sqrt{3}-t)}{(\sqrt{3}+t)(\sqrt{3}-t)}\right] d t=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}-t}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}+t}\right] d t$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{I}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot[\ln (\sqrt{3}+t)-\ln (\sqrt{3}-t)]_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+t}{\sqrt{3}-t}\right)\right]_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1$

$=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}-1}\right)-\ln 2\right]=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left\{\frac{2}{(\sqrt{3}-1)^2}\right\}-\ln 2\right]$

$\boldsymbol{I}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln \left\{\frac{2}{2(\sqrt{3}-1)^2}\right\}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln \left\{\frac{1}{(\sqrt{3}-1)^2}\right\}$

$=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln \left\{(\sqrt{3}-1)^{-2}\right\}=\left(-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln (\sqrt{3}-1) \ldots \ldots(i)$

$$\therefore $$ $ \alpha=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow 9 \alpha^2=12$

questions.questionText: Let $f$ be a polynomial function such that $\log _2(f(x))=\left(\log _2\left(2+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{9}+\ldots \ldots \infty\right)\right) \cdot \log _3\left(1+\frac{f(x)}{f(1 / x)}\right), x>0$ and $f(6)=37$. Then $\sum\limits_{\mathrm{n}=1}^{10} f(\mathrm{n})$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Statement of Work :

  1. 1. $\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})$ is a polynomial function

  2. 2. A logarithmic equation involving function $\left.f: \log _2[f(x)]=\left[\log _2\left(2+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{9}+\ldots . . \infty\right)\right] \cdot \log _3\left\{1+\frac{\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})}{f\left(\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}}\right)}\right\}\right] ; x>0$ and $\boldsymbol{f}(6)=37$

  3. 3. To evaluate the sum : $\sum\limits_{n=1}^{10} \boldsymbol{f}(n)$

$\begin{aligned} \log _2[\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})] & =\left[\log _2\left(2+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{9}+\ldots . \infty\right)\right] \cdot\left[\log _3\left\{1+\frac{\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})}{\boldsymbol{f}\left(\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}}\right)}\right\}\right] \\ & \left.=\left[\log _2 2\left\{1+\frac{1}{3}+\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2+\ldots . . \infty\right\}\right] \cdot \left\lvert\, \log _3\left\{1+\frac{\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})}{\boldsymbol{f}\left(\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}}\right)}\right\}\right.\right] . \\ & =\left[\log _2 2\left(\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{3}}\right)\right] \cdot\left[\log _3\left\{1+\frac{\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})}{\boldsymbol{f}\left(\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}}\right)}\right\}\right] \\ & \left.=\left[\log _2 3\right] \cdot \left\lvert\, \log _3\left\{1+\frac{\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})}{\boldsymbol{f}\left(\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}}\right)}\right\}\right.\right]\end{aligned}$

$\frac{\log _2[f(x)]}{\log _2 3}=\left[\log _3\left\{1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right)\right]$

$\Rightarrow \log _3[f(x)]=\log _3\left\{1+\frac{\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})}{f\left(\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{x}}\right)}\right\}$

$\Rightarrow f(x)=1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $f(x) \cdot f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=f(x)+f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)$ .....(i)

The function $\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})$ is a polynomial function stated in SOW where $\boldsymbol{f}(6)=37=(6)^2+1 \ldots . .(\boldsymbol{i i})$

Equation (ii) on logical analysis reduces the polynomial $\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})$ to the possible form below :

$f(x)=x^2+1 \ldots \ldots(i i i) \leftarrow$ Cubic powers of $x$ is not possible where $f(6)>216$

Now let us analyze the polynomial function $\boldsymbol{f}(\boldsymbol{x})$ from preconditions set in SOW :

$$f(x) \cdot f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\left(1+x^2\right) \cdot\left(1+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)=1+\frac{1}{x^2}+x^2+1=\left(1+x^2\right)+\left(1+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)=f(x)+f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) \ldots . (iv)$$

Equation $(i i),(i i i)$ and $(i v)$ mathematically certify the polynomial $f(x)=1+x^2$ :

$f(x)=1+x^2$

$\Rightarrow s_{10}=\sum\limits_{n=1}^{10} f(\boldsymbol{n})=\sum\limits_{\boldsymbol{n}=1}^{10}\left(1+\boldsymbol{n}^2\right)=\sum\limits_{\boldsymbol{n}=1}^{10}(1)+\sum\limits_{n=1}^{10}\left(n^2\right)$

$=10+\frac{(10) \cdot(10+1) \cdot\{(2) \cdot(10)+1\}}{6}=10+385=395$

questions.questionText:
The sum of squares of all the real solutions of the equation

$\log _{(x+1)}\left(2 x^2+5 x+3\right)=4-\log _{(2 x+3)}\left(x^2+2 x+1\right)$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\log _{(x+1)}\left(2 x^2+5 x+3\right)=4-\log _{(2 x+3)}\left(x^2+2 x+1\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\log _{(x+1)}(x+1)(2 x+3)=4-\log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\log _{(x+1)}(x+1)+\log _{(x+1)}(2 x+3)=4-2 \log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $1+\frac{1}{\log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)}=4-2 \log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)$

Substitute $\log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)=\boldsymbol{t}$

$$\therefore $$ $1+\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{t}}=4-2 \boldsymbol{t} \Rightarrow 2 \boldsymbol{t}^2-3 \boldsymbol{t}+1=0$

$\Rightarrow(\boldsymbol{t}-1)(2 \boldsymbol{t}-1)=0 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{t}=\left\{1, \frac{1}{2}\right\}$

For t = 1,

$\log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)=1 \Rightarrow 2 x+3=x+1 \Rightarrow x=-2$

For t = $\frac{1}{2}$,

$\log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow(x+1)^2=2 x+3 \Rightarrow x^2=2 \Rightarrow x= \pm \sqrt{2}$

$\sum\limits_{j=1}^3\left[x_j\right]^2=0+0+(\sqrt{2})^2=2$
